---
title: "Dessert Match-ups: Part II"
description: |
  Sweets on sweets on sweets
date: '2026-06-22'
image: "cookies.jpg"
categories:
    - Food
format: html
toc: true
lightbox: true
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  error: false
  message: false
draft: false
---

```{r}
library(here)
source(here("packages.R"))
source(here("functions.R"))

series_filter = 16

data = pollresults(series_filter)
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-overview
#| fig-cap: "Votes and shares for each question (faceted)."
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 10.5
faceted_bar_plot(data)
```

# How decisive were these results, combined?

```{r}
#| label: summary-stats
summary_d2 = summarize_two_option(data)
lopsided_d2 = summary_d2 %>% arrange(p_value_50_50)
closest_d2  = summary_d2 %>% arrange(desc(p_value_50_50)) %>% slice(1)
```

**TL;DR:** Ice Cream Sandwich buried Popsicle (`r lopsided_d2$leader_votes[1]`–`r lopsided_d2$trailer_votes[1]`, `r percent(lopsided_d2$leader_share[1],1)`), the most decisive result of this dessert round. Cinnamon, meanwhile, ended in a flat-out tie: Churro `r closest_d2$leader_votes[1]`, Cinnamon Bun `r closest_d2$trailer_votes[1]` — you can't get closer to a coin flip than that.

```{r}
#| label: summary-table
binom_summary_table(summary_d2)
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-leader-ci
#| fig-cap: "Leader shares with 95% confidence intervals."
#| fig-width: 7
#| fig-height: 4
leader_ci_plot(summary_d2)
```

# Does HQ agree with RTP?

```{r}
#| label: hq-rtp-stats
hq_rtp_d2 = hq_rtp_compare(data)
alignment_d2 = cor(hq_rtp_d2$hq_share, hq_rtp_d2$rtp_share)
cumulative_d2 = cumulative_alignment(16)

flips_d2 = data %>%
  group_by(question) %>%
  filter(n() == 2, sum(rtp_votes) > 0) %>%
  group_modify(~ {
    hq_winner  = .x$option[which.max(.x$hq_votes)]
    rtp_winner = .x$option[which.max(.x$rtp_votes)]
    tibble(hq_winner, rtp_winner, flipped = hq_winner != rtp_winner)
  }) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  filter(flipped)

flip_count_d2 = flip_count_so_far(16)
```

```{r}
#| label: hq-rtp-table
hq_rtp_d2 %>%
  transmute(
    question,
    `Leader (overall)` = option,
    `HQ share`  = sprintf("%s (%d/%d)", percent(hq_share, 1), hq_votes, hq_N),
    `RTP share` = sprintf("%s (%d/%d)", percent(rtp_share, 1), rtp_votes, rtp_N),
    `Gap (pts)` = percent(abs(gap), 1),
    `p-value`   = signif(p_value, 3)
  ) %>%
  kableExtra::kable()
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-hq-rtp
#| fig-cap: "HQ vs RTP support for each question's overall winner."
#| fig-width: 7
#| fig-height: 4
hq_rtp_plot(hq_rtp_d2)
```

The Alignment Index itself is unremarkable this week — **`r round(alignment_d2, 2)`**, basically flat versus last time, with the cumulative number at **`r round(cumulative_d2, 2)`**. But look at the flip count instead of the correlation: `r nrow(flips_d2)` of this poll's `r nrow(hq_rtp_d2)` questions — `r flips_d2$question %>% paste(collapse = ", ")` — saw HQ and RTP prefer genuinely different desserts. That's a majority of this week's questions, the most we've seen split by office in a single poll, and it brings the running tally to **`r flip_count_d2` flips** since RTP joined. Still nothing here clears statistical significance on its own (these are small offices, and small offices are noisy) — but a correlation coefficient can look perfectly fine while more than half the individual questions quietly disagree underneath it, which is exactly what's happening this week.
